📊 Module 1 · Business Excel

Getting Started at Nexus Dynamics

You are new. Karen has a project ready. Before the data starts coming in, you need to know your way around Excel.

▶ What this lesson covers (learning objectives, vocabulary, MOS objectives)

Skills you will build

  • Create, open, and save workbooks
  • Navigate the Ribbon and interface
  • Enter, edit, and clear cell data
  • Save files in alternate formats
  • Navigate to named cells and ranges
  • Use Find & Replace
  • Use AutoFill to complete data series

MOS Objectives (MO-200)

  • 1.2.1 — Navigate to named cells
  • 1.2.2 — Find and replace data
  • 1.5.2 — Save in alternate formats
  • 2.1.2 — Fill cells using AutoFill

Key vocabulary

  • Workbook · Worksheet · Cell · Range
  • Ribbon · Name Box · Formula Bar
  • AutoFill · Fill Handle · Named Range
📬

Hi — hope your first morning is going well.

1

The Excel Interface

Know your workspace before the data arrives.

🤔

Before we start — what do you already know?

Karen just asked you to find a specific cell. You open Excel and see a grid of rows and columns. What is the correct name for one single box in that grid?

0 of 3 sections read
📊
What is Excel — and why does it matter here?
Unread
▼
🎙️
Listen — Rachel, Instructor
What is Excel & Key Terms
0:00--:--

Microsoft Excel is a spreadsheetA spreadsheet is a grid-based application for organizing, calculating, and analyzing data. application — a tool for organizing data in rows and columns, performing calculations, and building reports. At Nexus Dynamics, it is how raw data gets cleaned, structured, and prepared before it reaches leadership.

When Karen says the Meridian files need work, she means the data exists but is not usable yet. Your job is to change that.

✅

Professional context: Excel fluency makes you immediately useful on any data team — it is why Karen noticed your resume.

Key terms

  • A workbookA workbook is an Excel file (.xlsx). It can contain multiple worksheets. is the Excel file — the .xlsx file Karen will send you.
  • A worksheetA worksheet is a single tab inside a workbook. is one tab inside the workbook — like a page in a notebook.
  • A cellA cell is the intersection of a row and a column. Each cell has a unique address like C3. is one box in the grid — identified by its column letter and row number (e.g. C3).
  • A rangeA range is a group of cells. A1:D20 refers to all cells from A1 to D20. is a group of cells, like A1:D20.
🎛
The Excel interface — annotated
Unread
▼

Karen asked you to find a cell and report back. Before you can do that, you need to know where everything lives. The diagram below (Figure 2 from the textbook) shows all the key elements of the Excel interface. Study it, then use the interactive legend to explore each element in detail.

📁
Creating and opening workbooks
Unread
▼

Create a new workbook

Go to File → New → Blank workbook or press Ctrl+N.

Open an existing workbook

Go to File → Open or press Ctrl+O. When Karen sends the Meridian files, this is how you open them.

Always save a working copy first

Open the original, then immediately use File → Save As (F12) to save a copy under a new name. Never work directly on the only copy of a source file.

⚠️

When Karen sends you the Meridian data files, save a working copy before making any changes. Always keep the original untouched.

🖥 Simulation — Navigate the Interface
1. Click cell C3
2. Read the Name Box
3. Click the Formulas tab
A1
fx
Karen asked you to check cell C3. Click on cell C3 in the grid below — this is the Operations sheet data. Watch the Name Box update.
🎉 You found it! Cell C3 contains "158,000" — the Q2 Revenue for Operations. You can now reply to Karen. Knowledge Check unlocked.
🏆

You can now navigate the Excel interface

You know what a workbook, worksheet, cell, and range are — and you can find your way around the Ribbon, Name Box, and Formula Bar.

✓ Added to your Function Reference Card

2

Entering & Editing Data

Karen has corrections. You need to know how to put data in — and fix it when it is wrong.

🔁

Connecting to Part 1

In Part 1 you learned that a cell is where data lives. Every name, number, and date in the Meridian file sits in a cell. Now you will learn how to put data there, fix it when it is wrong, and save the result.

🤔

Think it through first

You click a cell and start typing a name. Halfway through you realize you picked the wrong cell. What is the fastest way to cancel without changing anything?

0 of 3 sections read
⌨️
Entering data — the basics
Unread
▼

Click a cell and start typing. Excel enters edit modeEdit mode is when a cell is active and ready for input. automatically.

Completing an entry

  • Press Enter — confirms and moves down one row
  • Press Tab — confirms and moves right one column
  • Press any arrow key — confirms and moves in that direction
  • Press Escape — cancels the entry, nothing is saved
✅

When filling a column like Karen's employee list, use Enter after each name — it drops you to the next row automatically.

Data types

  • Text — names, labels, categories. Aligns left.
  • Numbers — quantities, amounts. Aligns right.
  • Dates — enter as MM/DD/YYYY. Excel stores them as numbers.
✏️
Editing and clearing cell contents
Unread
▼

Two ways to edit

  • Full replace: Select the cell and start typing — replaces everything.
  • Partial edit: Double-click the cell or press F2 to put a cursor inside the existing content.

Clearing a cell

Press Delete to remove content while keeping formatting. To remove both, use Home → Clear → Clear All.

⚠️

Delete removes content, not the cell itself. To remove the cell and shift data, right-click → Delete.

💾
Saving your work
Unread
▼

Save vs. Save As

  • Ctrl+S — saves in current location with current name
  • F12 — opens Save As to choose a new name, location, or format

Alternate formats

  • .xlsx — Standard Excel workbook, keeps all formatting
  • .csv — Plain text, no formatting. Required for the HR system Karen mentioned.
  • .pdf — Fixed layout for sharing with people who should not edit the file
🔔

Saving as .csv removes all formatting and any sheets beyond the first. Always keep your .xlsx as the working copy.

🖥 Simulation — Correct the Employee Data
1. Enter 3 missing last names
2. Fix Carlos's department
3. Clear the placeholder in C7
A1
fx
Karen's corrections: Click cell A4 and type Rivera, press Enter. Then A5: Okafor, A6: Patel. Double-click to edit existing cells. Select a cell and press Delete to clear it.
🎉 Corrections complete. Knowledge Check unlocked.
🏆

You can now enter, edit, and save data in Excel

You can put data into cells, fix mistakes, clear content, and save in the format Karen needs.

Function Reference Card — Part 2

I want to cancel a cell entry mid-typing
→
Press Escape
I want to edit part of a cell without replacing it
→
Double-click cell or press F2
I want to remove cell content
→
Select cell → press Delete
I want to save a file as CSV or PDF
→
File → Save As → change file type

The quarterly headers and region labels need fixing
Karen Martinez
k.martinez@nexusdynamics.com
Today, 10:05 AM
To: You <analyst@nexusdynamics.com>

Good progress this morning. Two more things before this file is ready to share.

📋 Your task: The revenue summary has "Q1" in cell B1 but Q2, Q3, and Q4 are missing — the headers need to continue the pattern across to column E. Also, the region labels in the lower rows are inconsistent — some say "Meridian-East" — it needs to be standardized to "East Region" across all rows. There are two of them in there, so make sure you catch both. There are also named ranges in this workbook that I have set up — you should be able to jump directly to the SalesData range using the Name Box.

Once those are done the file will be in good shape for the team review on Friday.

Karen MartinezDirector of Business Operations · Nexus Dynamics
3

Navigating & Filling Cells

Hundreds of rows. Move fast, find inconsistencies, let Excel complete patterns.

🔁

Building on Parts 1 and 2

You can open workbooks and enter data. Karen's latest email has three tasks — filling a pattern, fixing inconsistent labels across many rows, and navigating directly to a named range. All three need new tools.

🤔

Think it through first

Rows in the Meridian file say "Meridian-East" but should say "East Region." There are two of them. How do you fix both across 400 rows. You need to fix all of them to "East Region." What is the most efficient approach?

0 of 4 sections read
🧭
Navigating large worksheets
Unread
▼

Scrolling through hundreds of rows is slow. Use these keyboard shortcuts instead:

  • Ctrl+End — jumps to the last cell with data
  • Ctrl+Home — returns to cell A1
  • Ctrl+Arrow — jumps to the last filled cell in that direction
  • Ctrl+G — opens Go To dialog

Fastest: click the Name Box, type an address like C400, press Enter. You are there instantly.

✅

Use Ctrl+End first on any new file to see exactly how large the dataset is.

🏷
Named ranges — navigate by meaning, not address
Unread
▼

A named rangeA named range is a cell or group of cells given a descriptive name like SalesData, in addition to its address. gives a cell or group of cells a descriptive name — like SalesData — that you can use instead of an address. Karen has already set these up in the Meridian workbook.

Navigate to a named range

Click the Name Box dropdown arrow to see all named ranges, then click one to jump there. Or type the name directly in the Name Box and press Enter.

💡

Named ranges also make formulas easier to read. =SUM(SalesData) is clearer than =SUM(B2:E5) — and it does not break if rows are added.

🔍
Find & Replace — fix inconsistencies at scale
Unread
▼

Karen's region label problem — "Meridian-East" appearing where it should say "East Region" — is exactly what Find & Replace is for. It fixes every instance at once.

How to use it

Press Ctrl+H. Enter the text to find and what to replace it with. Click Replace All.

Figure 5 & 6 The Find and Replace dialog — your actual Excel window
⚡
AutoFill — let Excel finish the pattern
Unread
▼

AutoFillAutoFill detects a pattern in your data and extends it when you drag the fill handle. detects a pattern and completes it automatically. Karen's missing Q2–Q4 headers are a perfect use case.

How to use it

  1. Enter the starting value (Q1 is already there)
  2. Select that cell
  3. Hover over the bottom-right corner until you see the fill handleThe fill handle is the small green square at the bottom-right corner of a selected cell. — a small green square
  4. Click and drag across the cells you want to fill

What AutoFill detects

  • Quarter labels: Q1 → Q2, Q3, Q4
  • Number series: 1, 2, 3 or 10, 20, 30
  • Dates: Monday, Tuesday… or Jan, Feb, Mar
Figure 7 AutoFill Options — appears after dragging the fill handle
Figure 8 Fill options from the Home tab — Editing group
✅

After dragging, the AutoFill Options button (⊕) appears — click it to choose between filling a series or copying the value (see Figure 7 above).

A1
